skill.name: protocolsio-integration
skill.description: Guidance for using the protocols.io API to manage scientific protocols, workspaces, discussions, files, authentication, and exports.
skill.summary: Manage scientific protocols and lab collaboration through the protocols.io API.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found Markdown API examples rather than bundled executable malware. The real risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill teaches authenticated network calls that can create, publish, delete, upload, download, and export protocols.io data.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Remote Data Modification
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated protocols.io operations that create protocols, add steps, publish protocols, and delete protocol steps. These are legitimate API workflows, but running them with real tokens can alter or publish remote research records.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly describe authenticated token use and write, publish, or delete endpoints. This is a clear operational risk, but the context is documented protocols.io integration rather than concealed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Workspace File and Export Operations
medium_findings.description: The file and organization workflows include uploading local files, downloading workspace files, deleting folders, and exporting organization data with files and comments. These actions can expose or alter sensitive research data if used with broad permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows concrete upload, download, delete, and export examples. The risk depends on user authorization and chosen identifiers, so it is elevated but not malicious by itself.

medium_findings.title: OAuth Tokens Grant Private Content Access
medium_findings.description: The authentication guidance explains tokens that access private content, client secrets, refresh tokens, and bearer authorization headers. Mishandling these values could expose private protocols or workspace data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token capabilities and secret fields are explicitly documented, and the file also gives appropriate security guidance. This supports medium risk with user safeguards, not a finding of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backtick and fenced-code examples as Ruby or shell execution. Review found Markdown documentation and curl examples for protocols.io endpoints, with no bundled script that automatically executes commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are clearly Markdown examples and not executable skill code. Users can still copy commands manually, so the pattern is not ignored entirely.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded protocols.io URLs Are Expected API Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to the documented protocols.io API base URL and official endpoints. I found no evidence that these URLs send data to an unrelated or suspicious host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All inspected URL examples use protocols.io API or protocols.io documentation endpoints. Network use is intentional for this integration and not hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Appear to Be Token Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to MD5, SHA-1, insecure random generation, or custom encryption in the reviewed context. The flagged areas discuss OAuth, bearer tokens, access tokens, and API parameters.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain authentication and error-handling documentation, not weak cryptographic algorithms. Confidence is below very high because I did not manually inspect every duplicate static location.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no evidence of override instructions, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis in the inspected skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common injection phrases returned no matches, and inspected files use normal documentation language. This is a negative finding, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token HTTP Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show requests that include bearer tokens in headers. This is required for the API, but tokens must not be exposed in prompts, logs, source control, or shared transcripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly show bearer authorization patterns. The pattern is dangerous only if real tokens are mishandled.

dangerous_patterns.title: Write and Publish API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes POST and PATCH workflows that create protocols, update metadata, add comments, record experiments, and publish protocols. Users should confirm identifiers and environment before executing them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples make authenticated write requests to protocols.io. The intent is legitimate documentation, but the operational effect can be significant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Upload and Remote Download Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read a local file for upload and download remote workspace files. This can move sensitive data between the local environment and protocols.io workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload and download flows are explicit and use authenticated workspace endpoints. The risk is data exposure from user-selected files rather than automatic filesystem scraping.

dangerous_patterns.title: Delete and Bulk Export Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference material includes delete endpoints and an organization export endpoint that can include files and comments. These operations need human confirmation and least-privilege credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited endpoints directly delete or export data. They are documented features, but their impact warrants warning in the marketplace listing.

content.value_statement: Scientific teams need reliable ways to manage protocols, workspace files, discussions, and experiment records. This skill provides structured guidance for using the protocols.io API with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Explains protocols.io authentication, OAuth token exchange, token refresh, and authorization headers.
Documents protocol search, creation, update, step management, publishing, DOI, and PDF workflows.
Guides workspace operations, member access concepts, workspace protocol lists, and team collaboration patterns.
Covers protocol comments, threaded replies, step-level discussions, and collaboration troubleshooting.
Describes file upload, download, metadata update, folder organization, deletion, and restore workflows.
Includes examples for profiles, notifications, experiment records, publications, and organization exports.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not provide a packaged executable client or verified SDK.
Users must supply valid protocols.io credentials and confirm their own permission scope.
API details may change, so critical workflows should be checked against current protocols.io documentation.
The examples can modify remote data when run with real tokens and production identifiers.

use_cases.title: Build a Lab Protocol Repository
use_cases.description: Create, organize, publish, and maintain protocols inside a shared protocols.io workspace.

use_cases.title: Integrate Protocol Data into Tools
use_cases.description: Use documented endpoints to search protocols, retrieve metadata, upload files, and export records.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Collaborative Review
use_cases.description: Monitor comments, collect feedback, update protocol steps, and document experiment outcomes.

prompt_templates.title: Find Relevant Protocols
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me search protocols.io for public protocols about [topic]. Include filters, pagination advice, and fields I should review before citing a protocol.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Protocol Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a protocols.io API workflow to create a protocol named [title], add [number] steps, attach materials, and keep it unpublished until review.

prompt_templates.title: Organize Workspace Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a protocols.io workspace file workflow for [project]. Include folder structure, upload metadata, verification, and permission checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Protocol Activity
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a protocols.io integration that exports organization data, reviews workspace activity, summarizes discussions, and flags outdated protocols without exposing private tokens.

output_examples.input: Search for CRISPR protocols and decide which ones to review.
output_examples.output: A search plan with recommended filters, pagination settings, review fields, DOI checks, and a short protocol comparison summary.

output_examples.input: Upload experiment results to a workspace and link them to a run record.
output_examples.output: A workflow covering folder selection, file metadata, upload verification, experiment record fields, and follow-up permission checks.

output_examples.input: Prepare a team review process for a draft protocol.
output_examples.output: A collaboration checklist with comment monitoring, step-level feedback, revision tracking, and publication readiness criteria.

best_practices: Store access tokens outside prompts, source files, notebooks, and shared transcripts.
Test write, delete, publish, and export workflows in a non-production workspace first.
Confirm workspace permissions and data sensitivity before uploading files or exporting organization data.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real access tokens into examples, issue trackers, or shared chat sessions.
Do not run delete, publish, or export examples against production identifiers without human confirmation.
Do not assume public protocol access rules apply to private workspaces or institutional data.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to protocols.io by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users or tools must make API requests with valid credentials.

faq.question: Can it access private protocols?
faq.answer: Private access depends on the token scope, workspace permissions, and protocols.io account settings.

faq.question: Does it include OAuth guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains authorization links, token exchange, token refresh, state parameters, and redirect URI checks.

faq.question: Can it upload and delete files?
faq.answer: The documentation includes upload, metadata update, download, delete, restore, and folder management API workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for regulated research data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review token scope, workspace permissions, institutional policy, and data sensitivity before using export or upload workflows.

faq.question: Which AI tools are listed as compatible?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
